findings.title: SES SendEmail policy allows wildcard resource
findings.description: The lambda_admin_exec role has an IAM policy that allows ses:SendEmail action with Resource set to '*' (wildcard). This is overly permissive. The policy should be restricted to specific verified email identities or Amazon SES sender addresses.
findings.fix_suggestion: Restrict the Resource to specific verified email identities: { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": ["ses:SendEmail"], "Resource": "arn:aws:ses:${var.aws_region}:${data.aws_caller_identity.current.account_id}:identity/${var.noreply_email}" }
findings.evidence: { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": ["ses:SendEmail"], "Resource": "*" }
findings.impact: An attacker who gains access to the admin Lambda function could send emails from any identity within the AWS account, potentially for phishing or spam campaigns.

findings.title: DynamoDB ratelimit table missing point-in-time recovery
findings.description: The photo-gallery-ratelimit DynamoDB table does not have point_in_time_recovery enabled. While this table contains ephemeral data (rate limit counters with TTL), enabling PITR provides protection against accidental or malicious deletion and allows recovery to any point in the last 35 days.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add point_in_time_recovery block: point_in_time_recovery { enabled = true }
findings.evidence: resource aws_dynamodb_table ratelimit { name = photo-gallery-ratelimit, billing_mode = PAY_PER_REQUEST, hash_key = pk, ... # Missing: point_in_time_recovery block }
findings.impact: Without point-in-time recovery, accidental deletion of rate limit data or a malicious table wipe cannot be recovered. While the data is ephemeral and TTL-based, this adds operational risk.

findings.title: DynamoDB sub_admins table missing point-in-time recovery
findings.description: The photo-gallery-sub-admins DynamoDB table does not have point_in_time_recovery enabled. This table stores admin/team member account data, which is critical for business continuity. Without PITR, accidental or malicious deletion of admin accounts cannot be recovered.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add point_in_time_recovery block: point_in_time_recovery { enabled = true }
findings.evidence: resource aws_dynamodb_table sub_admins { name = photo-gallery-sub-admins, billing_mode = PAY_PER_REQUEST, hash_key = admin_id, ... # Missing: point_in_time_recovery block }
findings.impact: Accidental or malicious deletion of admin account records cannot be recovered, potentially locking out gallery owners from their admin panel and causing service outage.

findings.title: CodeBuild IAM role has wildcard permissions for IAM and API Gateway
findings.description: The CodeBuild deployment role (via the codebuild module) has overly permissive IAM policies. Action 'iam:*' on resource '*' allows full IAM control (create/delete roles, modify policies, etc.). Action 'apigateway:*' on resource '*' allows unrestricted API Gateway modifications. These could be constrained to specific resource ARNs and actions.
findings.fix_suggestion: Constrain IAM actions to specific role names: Action = ["iam:GetRole", "iam:TagRole", "iam:PassRole"]; Resource = "arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/auth-service-*"
findings.evidence: IAM policy with 'iam:*' and 'apigateway:*' actions on resource '*'
findings.impact: Overprivileged CodeBuild role increases blast radius if CodeBuild container is compromised. Could allow attacker to modify any IAM role or API Gateway in the account.

findings.title: API Gateway CORS allows localhost development origins in production config
findings.description: The CORS configuration allows HTTP connections from localhost:3000 and localhost:3001. These are development origins and should not be in production Terraform configuration.
findings.fix_suggestion: Use environment-specific variables: allow_origins = var.environment == "production" ? [production_origins] : [dev_origins]
findings.evidence: allow_origins = [..., "http://localhost:3000", "http://localhost:3001"]
findings.impact: Localhost origins in production allow development/local requests to the auth API. Could be exploited if developer machine is compromised.

findings.title: S3 Terraform state backend lacks DynamoDB locking table
findings.description: The Terraform backend uses S3 without a DynamoDB table for state locking. Without locking, concurrent terraform apply operations can corrupt the state file.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks" and encrypt = true to backend block
findings.evidence: backend "s3" { bucket = "photo-gallery-tfstate-229678440188"; key = "auth-service/terraform.tfstate"; region = "us-west-2" }
findings.impact: Risk of Terraform state corruption if two deployments run concurrently. Could cause infrastructure inconsistency or failed deployments.

findings.title: Missing CloudWatch alarms for Lambda errors and throttling
findings.description: The Lambda function has no CloudWatch alarms for errors, throttling, or timeouts. Production incidents will go unnoticed.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm resources for Errors, Throttles, and Duration metrics
findings.evidence: resource "aws_lambda_function" "api" { ... } (no alarms defined)
findings.impact: Production incidents (errors, throttling) will not trigger alerts. Errors could accumulate undetected, degrading service quality.

findings.title: No CloudWatch alarms for API Gateway errors and latency
findings.description: API Gateway stage has no alarms for 4xx/5xx errors or high latency. API errors go unnoticed.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm resources for 4XXError, 5XXError, and Latency
findings.evidence: resource "aws_apigatewayv2_stage" "default" { ... } (no alarms)
findings.impact: API errors and degradation go unnoticed. Customers may experience failed requests while operators are unaware.

findings.title: Lambda reserved concurrent executions (20) may be over-provisioned
findings.description: Lambda reserves 20 concurrent executions which costs ~$11/month even when not in use. For variable traffic, this may be unnecessary.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove or lower reservation: reserved_concurrent_executions = null or = 10 based on actual traffic patterns
findings.evidence: reserved_concurrent_executions = 20
findings.impact: Wasted cost ~$132/year if typical traffic is <20 concurrent executions.

findings.title: No custom error responses defined for API Gateway
findings.description: API Gateway returns generic AWS error messages instead of application-specific responses.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add custom_error_response blocks or document that clients handle generic errors
findings.evidence: resource "aws_apigatewayv2_stage" "default" (no custom_error_response)
findings.impact: Clients receive generic AWS error messages (502 Bad Gateway) instead of application-specific details.

findings.title: DynamoDB table lacks explicit server-side encryption configuration
findings.description: The DynamoDB table uses implicit AWS-managed encryption but best practice is to specify explicitly.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add server_side_encryption { enabled = true } block
findings.evidence: resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "data" (no server_side_encryption block)
findings.impact: Implicit encryption is fine but does not enforce encryption in code review.

findings.title: DynamoDB point-in-time recovery (PITR) enables extra backup costs
findings.description: PITR is enabled which costs additional storage (~$0.25/GB/month). Verify this is needed for compliance or recovery SLA.
findings.fix_suggestion: Disable if not required: point_in_time_recovery { enabled = false } or document compliance reason
findings.evidence: point_in_time_recovery { enabled = true }
findings.impact: Extra storage costs for backups that may not be necessary for low-volume auth table.

findings.title: No Lambda function versioning or aliases for deployment safety
findings.description: Lambda function is deployed without versioning or aliases. No rollback capability or canary deployment strategy.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add aws_lambda_function_version and aws_lambda_alias resources for safe deployments
findings.evidence: resource "aws_lambda_function" "api" (no version or alias)
findings.impact: No rollback capability if deployment is bad. Function failures are not recoverable to previous version.

findings.title: Lambda reserved concurrency blocks scaling and wastes capacity
findings.description: Reserved concurrency = 20 prevents scaling beyond 20 concurrent executions. Traffic spikes above 20 will be throttled.
findings.fix_suggestion: Lower to expected peak (e.g., 10) or remove entirely. Monitor CloudWatch concurrency metrics.
findings.evidence: reserved_concurrent_executions = 20
findings.impact: Traffic spikes above 20 concurrent will receive 429 errors. Reserved capacity sits idle if traffic <20.

findings.title: Terraform backend not explicitly configured
findings.description: The Terraform configuration lacks an explicit backend block. This means the state file is stored locally by default, which is problematic for team environments and CI/CD pipelines. Multiple team members or CI systems may have conflicting state versions, causing Apply failures or data loss. For a multi-developer project with overnight autonomous loops, explicit S3 backend with state locking is essential.
findings.evidence: No 'terraform { backend "s3" { ... } }' block found. State stored locally by default.
findings.impact: State conflicts in team environments, inability to safely run parallel Apply operations, risk of state corruption during overnight autonomous loop, difficulty in CI/CD integration.

findings.title: No disaster recovery for dashboard data bucket
findings.description: The S3 bucket storing aggregated dashboard data has no cross-region replication or backup strategy. While versioning is enabled, a regional outage or accidental bucket deletion would make the dashboard unavailable. The dashboard is the operational hub for the entire system, making it critical infrastructure.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add cross-region replication configuration with replica bucket in another region (us-east-1 for example).
findings.evidence: aws_s3_bucket 'dashboard_data' has versioning and lifecycle rules, but no replication_configuration block or cross-region failover setup.
findings.impact: Total loss of dashboard data and operational visibility in case of regional outage, orphaned CloudFront distribution unable to serve cached content, loss of audit history and score trends.

findings.title: MFA Delete not enabled on S3 bucket
findings.description: The dashboard_data S3 bucket has versioning enabled but MFA Delete is not configured. This means versioned objects can be deleted without MFA requirement, increasing risk of accidental or malicious data loss. For a critical audit system, versioned data should be protected by MFA.
findings.fix_suggestion: Enable MFA Delete (requires root account configuration). Update versioning_configuration to include mfa_delete = "Enabled".
findings.evidence: aws_s3_bucket_versioning has 'status = "Enabled"' but no mfa_delete configuration.
findings.impact: Accidental or malicious deletion of critical audit data without additional authentication barrier, loss of audit trail integrity.

findings.title: Module source not pinned to exact commit
findings.description: The CodeBuild module is sourced from git with a ref constraint (codebuild-module-v1), but this references a tag/branch rather than a specific commit SHA. If the tag is reassigned or the branch advances, the same Terraform plan may pull different code in the future, causing unpredictable behavior.
findings.fix_suggestion: Pin to a specific commit SHA instead of tag: ?ref=<COMMIT_SHA>
findings.evidence: module source is 'git::https://github.com/CodyJo/codyjo.com.git//terraform/modules/codebuild?ref=codebuild-module-v1' — ref points to tag, not commit SHA.
findings.impact: Terraform plan may reference different module code on subsequent runs, breaking reproducibility, making overnight autonomous loop behavior unpredictable.

findings.title: S3 bucket uses AES256 instead of KMS-managed encryption
findings.description: The dashboard_data bucket uses AES256 server-side encryption (AWS-managed keys) instead of KMS-managed keys. While AES256 is sufficient for many use cases, KMS provides better key management, audit trails, and compliance with stricter standards like ISO 27001.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Create a KMS key and use it for S3 encryption:

resource "aws_kms_key" "dashboard_data" {
  description             = "KMS key for dashboard S3"
  deletion_window_in_days = 30
  enable_key_rotation     = true
}

Then update encryption config to use sse_kms_master_key_id.
findings.evidence: aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration uses 'sse_algorithm = "AES256"' instead of 'sse_kms_master_key_id'.
findings.impact: Weaker compliance posture, no audit trail of key usage, inability to rotate encryption keys independently.

findings.title: No CloudWatch alarms for CodeBuild failures
findings.description: The CodeBuild CI/CD pipeline for the overnight autonomous loop has no CloudWatch alarms configured. Build failures, timeouts, or throttling issues will not trigger alerts, potentially leaving broken infrastructure undetected.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add CloudWatch metric alarms for FailedBuilds >= 1, with SNS topic for alerting.
findings.evidence: Module 'codebuild' is referenced but no aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm resources are defined for build failures or timeouts.
findings.impact: Silent failures in overnight loop CI/CD, undetected broken deployments, no operational visibility into system health.

findings.title: No monitoring for S3 dashboard data bucket health
findings.description: The dashboard_data bucket has no CloudWatch alarms for unusual activity, failed requests, or 4xx/5xx errors. Silent data corruption or access issues could go undetected.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add S3 bucket metrics and CloudWatch alarms for 4xxErrors, 5xxErrors >= threshold.
findings.evidence: No aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm resources defined for S3 bucket metrics (replication failures, 4xx/5xx errors).
findings.impact: Undetected S3 replication failures, silent data access issues, no visibility into bucket health.

findings.title: Hardcoded S3 bucket and CloudFront distribution IDs in IAM policy
findings.description: The CodeBuild IAM policy hardcodes the S3 bucket name and CloudFront distribution ID as specific ARNs. This reduces flexibility and makes the policy brittle. Additionally, the S3 bucket ARN (admin-thenewbeautifulme-site) suggests this policy may be shared with another project.
findings.fix_suggestion: Extract bucket name and distribution ID as Terraform variables, then reference via interpolation.
findings.evidence: IAM policy has hardcoded ARNs: 'arn:aws:s3:::admin-thenewbeautifulme-site' and 'arn:aws:cloudfront::*:distribution/E372ZR95FXKVT5'
findings.impact: Silent permission failures if bucket or distribution ID changes, reduced maintainability, potential cross-project permission leakage.

findings.title: Missing cost allocation tags (Environment, CostCenter)
findings.description: The Terraform configuration applies only 'Project' and 'ManagedBy' tags via default_tags. Production systems should also tag with Environment, CostCenter, and Owner for cost allocation.
findings.fix_suggestion: Expand default_tags to include Environment, CostCenter, Owner variables.
findings.evidence: default_tags contains only: Project and ManagedBy. Missing: Environment, CostCenter, Owner.
findings.impact: Inability to allocate S3/CloudFront/CodeBuild costs to business units, difficulty in cost reports.

findings.title: No explicit CloudWatch log group retention
findings.description: CodeBuild automatically creates CloudWatch log groups, but no retention_in_days is configured. Logs will be retained indefinitely, accumulating storage costs.
findings.fix_suggestion: If the module supports it, pass log_retention_in_days = 30 (or desired retention period).
findings.evidence: Module 'codebuild' is sourced but no log_retention_in_days configuration is visible.
findings.impact: Unbounded CloudWatch Logs costs, accumulation of obsolete build logs.

findings.title: S3 lifecycle rule could be more aggressive
findings.description: The lifecycle rule expires old versions after 30 days. For a dashboard system with automated data refresh, old versions could expire sooner (e.g., 7 days) to reduce storage costs.
findings.fix_suggestion: Reduce noncurrent_version_expiration to 7 days: noncurrent_days = 7
findings.evidence: noncurrent_version_expiration { noncurrent_days = 30 }
findings.impact: Slightly higher S3 storage costs than necessary, accumulation of older dashboard snapshots.

findings.title: Dashboard domains variable not used
findings.description: The 'dashboard_domains' variable in variables.tf is defined but not referenced anywhere in the configuration. This suggests incomplete implementation or dead code.
findings.fix_suggestion: Either implement CloudFront distribution creation using dashboard_domains, or remove the unused variable.
findings.evidence: Variable 'dashboard_domains' is defined with default = [] but no resource references it.
findings.impact: Dead code in Terraform configuration, confusion for maintainers about intended functionality.

findings.title: S3 site bucket missing lifecycle policy for versioned objects
findings.description: The certstudy-site S3 bucket has versioning enabled but no lifecycle policy to expire old versions. This causes accumulated storage costs from non-current versions that accumulate indefinitely.
findings.impact: Each deployment creates a new version; old versions persist indefinitely, increasing S3 storage costs. For a high-velocity deployment pipeline, this can grow into a significant cost.

findings.title: Resend API key passed as plaintext Lambda environment variable
findings.description: The RESEND_API_KEY is passed to Lambda functions as a plaintext environment variable instead of being stored in Secrets Manager. Environment variables are visible in the Lambda console and CloudTrail logs, creating a credential exposure risk.
findings.impact: The Resend API key (used for transactional email) is visible in plaintext in AWS console, CloudTrail logs, and deployment logs. An attacker with Lambda console access or log access could extract the key and impersonate email sender.

findings.title: CodeBuild CloudFront invalidation permission uses wildcard resource
findings.description: The CodeBuild deploy policy allows cloudfront:CreateInvalidation and cloudfront:ListDistributions with Resource = '*'. This is broader than necessary and violates the principle of least privilege.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Scope the resource to only the certstudy distribution:

{
  Sid    = "CloudFront"
  Effect = "Allow"
  Action = ["cloudfront:CreateInvalidation", "cloudfront:ListDistributions"]
  Resource = aws_cloudfront_distribution.site.arn
}
findings.impact: The CodeBuild role can list and invalidate any CloudFront distribution in the account. If the CodeBuild pipeline is compromised, an attacker could disrupt other projects' CDNs.

findings.title: Lambda functions lack dead-letter queue (DLQ) configuration
findings.description: The three Lambda functions (api, tutor, planner) integrated with API Gateway lack DLQ configuration. When invocations fail, the requests are silently dropped with no way to replay or analyze them.
findings.evidence:
resource "aws_apigatewayv2_integration" "api" {
  api_id                 = aws_apigatewayv2_api.api.id
  integration_type       = "AWS_PROXY"
  integration_uri        = aws_lambda_function.api.invoke_arn
  payload_format_version = "2.0"
  # No dead_letter_config
}
findings.impact: Failed API requests that exhaust Lambda retries are lost. Users receive 5xx errors but there's no record of what failed or ability to replay the request. This reduces observability and recovery options.

findings.title: Missing CloudWatch alarms for Lambda errors and API Gateway throttling
findings.description: No CloudWatch alarms are defined for critical metrics: Lambda function error count, API Gateway 4xx/5xx errors, throttle events, or DynamoDB consumed capacity. Issues go unnoticed until customer-reported.
findings.impact: API outages, Lambda throttles, and errors are not detected proactively. Response time to incidents is delayed. No metric-based scaling or auto-remediation possible.

findings.title: Missing Terraform state locking configuration
findings.description: Terraform state is stored in S3 with no DynamoDB table for state locking. Concurrent runs (e.g., CI/CD + manual apply) can cause state corruption or race conditions.
findings.impact: If two Terraform runs execute simultaneously (e.g., local apply while CI/CD deploys), they may both read the same state version and create conflicting updates. This can result in infrastructure inconsistency, lost changes, or phantom resources.

findings.title: Inconsistent tagging across some resources
findings.description: While the provider has default_tags, some resources (like CloudWatch log group for API Gateway) do not explicitly add custom tags beyond defaults. The access logs bucket lacks environment/cost center tags.
findings.impact: Cost allocation and resource tracking are incomplete. AWS Cost Explorer cannot easily filter by component or cost center. Resource audit trails are harder to follow.

findings.title: Authentication secret embedded in Lambda source code
findings.description: The admin.codyjo.com Lambda@Edge function contains a hardcoded SHA256 hash of the HTTP Basic Authentication password in source code (admin-edge-auth/index.mjs line 3). While the actual password is not visible, the hash is version-controlled and could be subject to offline brute-force attacks. Any compromise of the repository exposes the authentication mechanism.
findings.fix_suggestion: Store the password hash in AWS Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store (similar to the GitHub PAT approach in codebuild-credential.tf). Retrieve it at Lambda runtime from the secret store instead of hardcoding. Example: fetch from Secrets Manager during Lambda initialization.
findings.evidence: const expected = '84f98956d19fc3aa729ff891c59bd0af18e86af2fdba24554ae3c09740d4b2db';
findings.impact: Compromised repository = compromised admin dashboard access. Attackers with repo access can brute-force the hash offline or redeploy malicious Lambda code.

findings.title: IAM policy grants s3:* wildcard on admin bucket
findings.description: The CodeBuild CD role's deploy policy (codebuild.tf lines 45-49) grants 's3:*' (all S3 actions) on the admin bucket and its contents. This violates the principle of least privilege. The role only needs PutObject, GetObject, DeleteObject, and ListBucket for deployment.
findings.fix_suggestion: Replace 's3:*' with specific actions: ["s3:PutObject", "s3:GetObject", "s3:DeleteObject", "s3:ListBucket"]. Same pattern already used for www.codyjo.com bucket in the S3Deploy statement.
findings.impact: Over-privileged role increases blast radius if CodeBuild credentials are compromised. Attacker could delete bucket versioning, modify bucket policies, or change encryption settings.

findings.title: IAM policy grants cloudfront:* with wildcard resources
findings.description: The CodeBuild CD role's deploy policy (codebuild.tf lines 52-63) grants all CloudFront actions ('cloudfront:*') on all resources ('*'). This is overly permissive. The role only needs to manage two specific distributions: the main site and the admin dashboard.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Scope resources to specific distribution ARNs:
"resources": [
  "arn:aws:cloudfront::<account>:distribution/EF4U8A7W3OH5K",
  "arn:aws:cloudfront::<account>:distribution/<admin-distribution-id>"
]
For ListDistributions and List* operations, use "*" only for those specific actions.
findings.evidence:
statement {
  sid    = "AdminCloudFront"
  effect = "Allow"
  actions = [
    "cloudfront:GetDistribution", "cloudfront:GetDistributionConfig",
    "cloudfront:UpdateDistribution", "cloudfront:CreateInvalidation",
    "cloudfront:ListDistributions", "cloudfront:GetOriginAccessControl",
    "cloudfront:ListOriginAccessControls", "cloudfront:GetResponseHeadersPolicy",
    "cloudfront:ListResponseHeadersPolicies", "cloudfront:TagResource",
    "cloudfront:ListTagsForResource",
  ]
  resources = ["*"]
}
findings.impact: Compromised role could modify or delete any CloudFront distribution in the AWS account, not just codyjo.com distributions. Could redirect traffic, inject malicious content, or cause DDoS.

findings.title: IAM policy grants acm:* with wildcard resources
findings.description: The CodeBuild CD role's deploy policy (codebuild.tf lines 73-80) grants all ACM actions ('acm:*') on all resources ('*'). This allows the role to request, delete, or manage any certificate in the account, beyond the scope of codyjo.com.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Scope resources to the specific certificate ARNs for codyjo.com and admin.codyjo.com:
"resources": [
  "arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:<account>:certificate/<admin-cert-id>",
  "arn:aws:acm:us-west-2:<account>:certificate/<www-cert-id>"
]
For ListTagsForCertificate, resources can be "*" as AWS ACM requires.
findings.impact: Compromised role could delete or revoke SSL/TLS certificates for other domains/applications, causing downtime.

findings.title: IAM policy grants lambda:* wildcard actions
findings.description: The CodeBuild CD role's deploy policy (codebuild.tf lines 83-90) grants all Lambda actions ('lambda:*') on the admin-edge-auth Lambda. This allows the role to delete the function, modify environment variables, or change the runtime. Only UpdateFunctionCode, PublishVersion, and a few read operations are needed for deployment.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Restrict to deployment-specific actions:
"actions": [
  "lambda:UpdateFunctionCode",
  "lambda:PublishVersion",
  "lambda:GetFunction",
  "lambda:GetFunctionCodeSigningConfig",
  "lambda:ListVersionsByFunction"
]
findings.impact: Over-privileged role increases risk of unauthorized Lambda modifications. Attacker could change function code, delete the function entirely, or inject malicious logic.

findings.title: No CloudWatch alarms for Lambda@Edge errors
findings.description: The admin-edge-auth Lambda@Edge function (admin.tf lines 33-43) has no CloudWatch alarms monitoring for execution errors or throttles. If authentication fails silently, the admin dashboard could become inaccessible without immediate notification.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Add CloudWatch alarms for Lambda@Edge metrics:
- aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm for Errors (lambda function-level CloudWatch metrics)
- aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm for Throttles
- Set alarm actions to SNS topic for notification
Note: Lambda@Edge metrics appear in CloudWatch under us-east-1 region.
findings.evidence:
resource "aws_lambda_function" "admin_edge_auth" {
  provider         = aws.us_east_1
  function_name    = "codyjo-admin-edge-auth"
  role             = aws_iam_role.admin_edge_auth.arn
  filename         = data.archive_file.admin_edge_auth.output_path
  source_code_hash = data.archive_file.admin_edge_auth.output_base64sha256
  handler          = "index.handler"
  runtime          = "nodejs18.x"
  publish          = true
  timeout          = 5
}
findings.impact: Silent failures in authentication logic could block admin dashboard access until manually investigated. No automated alerting to on-call team.

findings.title: No CloudWatch alarms for CodeBuild failures
findings.description: CodeBuild projects (modules/codebuild/main.tf lines 92-171) are configured with CloudWatch log groups but no alarms for failed builds. If the CI/CD pipeline fails silently, deployments could be missed.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Add CloudWatch alarms:
- aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm for CodeBuild FailedBuilds metric
- aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm for CodeBuild Throttled metric
- Connect to SNS topic for Slack/email notifications
Example:
metric_name = "FailedBuilds"
namespace = "AWS/CodeBuild"
dimensions = { ProjectName = aws_codebuild_project.cd.name }
threshold = 1
comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
findings.impact: Failed builds are logged but not actively monitored. Team may not notice CI/CD pipeline failures until end-user impact or manual build status check.

findings.title: Missing resource tags for cost allocation and management
findings.description: AWS resources (S3 buckets, Lambda, CloudFront, CloudWatch) lack tags for cost allocation, environment identification, and operational tracking. The Back Office project uses tags (Project, Environment, CostCenter) in other repos but not here.
findings.impact: Difficulty tracking costs per project. Harder to enforce tagging policies for governance and cost center allocation.

findings.title: Outdated Lambda runtime nodejs18.x
findings.description: The admin-edge-auth Lambda (admin.tf line 40) uses nodejs18.x runtime, which will be deprecated by AWS on 2024-09-08 (EOL passed). AWS recommends upgrading to nodejs20.x or nodejs22.x.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Update runtime to nodejs22.x:
runtime = "nodejs22.x"

Verify the Lambda function code (index.mjs) is compatible with Node.js 22 (it should be—only uses crypto and standard features). Test in development first.
findings.evidence: runtime = "nodejs18.x"
findings.impact: After EOL, AWS may stop accepting updates, disable function creation, or remove support. Migration to newer runtime will eventually be forced.

findings.title: S3 site bucket missing server-side encryption
findings.description: The aws_s3_bucket.site bucket does not have server-side encryption configured. While event_intake bucket has AES256 encryption enabled, the public-facing site bucket lacks encryption at rest, which is a security best practice for all S3 buckets regardless of access patterns.
findings.impact: Unencrypted data at rest exposes sensitive content. Although CloudFront is the primary access vector, direct S3 API access or AWS breach scenarios could expose unencrypted objects.

findings.title: CloudFront invalidation IAM policy uses wildcard resource
findings.description: The CodeBuild deploy policy allows cloudfront:CreateInvalidation and cloudfront:ListDistributions with Resource = "*" (all resources). This violates the principle of least privilege by allowing the CD role to invalidate any CloudFront distribution in the account, not just the cordivent site distribution.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Scope the policy to specific CloudFront distribution ARN:
{
  Sid      = "CloudFront"
  Effect   = "Allow"
  Action   = ["cloudfront:CreateInvalidation"]
  Resource = aws_cloudfront_distribution.site.arn
}
findings.impact: A compromise of the CodeBuild credentials could allow an attacker to invalidate cache on unrelated CloudFront distributions, causing service disruption across the account.

findings.title: CloudFront CSP policy allows unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval scripts
findings.description: The Content-Security-Policy header in the CloudFront response headers policy allows 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' for script-src. This significantly weakens CSP protections against XSS attacks, as it allows arbitrary inline scripts and dynamic code execution.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Remove 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' from script-src, ensuring application uses external scripts or nonce-based CSP:
content_security_policy = "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://analytics.codyjo.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; ..."
findings.evidence: content_security_policy = "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' ..."
findings.impact: Reduces effectiveness of CSP as an XSS defense mechanism. Any XSS vulnerability in the application cannot be mitigated by CSP, allowing attackers to inject and execute arbitrary JavaScript in users' browsers.

findings.title: Missing CloudWatch alarms for Lambda function metrics
findings.description: No CloudWatch alarms are configured to monitor Lambda function errors, throttles, duration, or concurrent executions. This means operational issues (failures, throttling, performance degradation) may not be detected until users report problems.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add CloudWatch alarms for each Lambda function for errors, duration, and throttles.
findings.evidence:
# aws_lambda_function resources defined without accompanying cloudwatch_metric_alarm resources
# No alarms for: Errors, Duration, Throttles, ConcurrentExecutions, UnreservedConcurrentExecutions
findings.impact: Operational blind spot for Lambda function health. Failed invocations, throttled requests, and performance regressions won't trigger alerts, leading to longer MTTR and potential revenue impact.

findings.title: Multiple Lambda functions share single IAM role without least privilege
findings.description: The api, interpret, and builder Lambda functions all share aws_iam_role.lambda with identical permissions. While individual function roles aren't strictly required if they genuinely need the same permissions, the combined permissions are broader than ideal for individual function requirements.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Create role-specific policies based on actual requirements:
- api-lambda role: DynamoDB, JWT secret, Resend API key, S3 event-intake, builder invoke
- interpret-lambda role: DynamoDB, Anthropic secret, JWT secret
- builder-lambda role: DynamoDB, Anthropic secret, S3 event-intake
findings.impact: If one Lambda function is compromised, attacker gains permissions of all three functions. Reduces blast radius containment.

findings.title: Lambda DynamoDB policy includes Scan action without restriction
findings.description: The Lambda execution role policy includes dynamodb:Scan action on the main DynamoDB table. Scan is often unnecessary and can lead to expensive, unexpected queries.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Remove Scan from the policy if not used:
Action = [
  "dynamodb:GetItem",
  "dynamodb:PutItem",
  "dynamodb:UpdateItem",
  "dynamodb:DeleteItem",
  "dynamodb:BatchWriteItem",
  "dynamodb:Query"
]
findings.evidence:
Action = [
  "dynamodb:GetItem",
  "dynamodb:PutItem",
  "dynamodb:UpdateItem",
  "dynamodb:DeleteItem",
  "dynamodb:BatchWriteItem",
  "dynamodb:Query",
  "dynamodb:Scan"
]
findings.impact: Developers might use Scan operations for convenience, leading to cost overruns and potential data exposure.

findings.title: EventBridge target for scheduler Lambda missing retry policy and DLQ
findings.description: The aws_cloudwatch_event_target for the scheduler function does not configure retry_policy or dead_letter_config. If an invocation fails, it's silently dropped.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add retry policy and DLQ configuration to the EventBridge target.
findings.impact: Failed scheduler invocations (email sending failures) are lost with no visibility.

findings.title: CloudFront cache configuration uses deprecated forwarded_values pattern
findings.description: The CloudFront distribution uses the older forwarded_values block instead of the recommended cache_policy_id and origin_request_policy_id pattern. This is deprecated and harder to maintain.
findings.fix_suggestion: Migrate to cache_policy_id pattern using aws_cloudfront_cache_policy.
findings.impact: Technical debt. Harder to troubleshoot and maintain than modern pattern.

findings.title: S3 site bucket missing lifecycle configuration for versioned objects
findings.description: The site bucket has versioning enabled but lacks a lifecycle policy to expire old versions, leading to unbounded storage costs.
findings.fix_suggestion: Add lifecycle configuration to expire old versions after 7 days.
findings.impact: Storage costs for old object versions accumulate over time.

findings.title: No reserved concurrent executions set on Lambda functions
findings.description: The Lambda functions do not set reserved_concurrent_executions, meaning they share the account-level concurrency limit.
findings.fix_suggestion: Set reserved concurrent executions based on expected peak load (e.g., 100 for api, 50 for interpret, 50 for builder).
findings.impact: In the absence of reserved concurrency, all functions compete for the account's concurrent execution limit, risking throttling.

findings.title: Lambda environment variables contain empty placeholder values for push notifications
findings.description: The API Lambda function defines VAPID_PUBLIC_KEY and VAPID_PRIVATE_KEY as empty strings, presumably as placeholders for Web Push support.
findings.fix_suggestion: Remove these variables if push notifications aren't currently used.
findings.impact: Empty environment variables can cause confusion about feature status.

findings.title: DynamoDB GSI uses ProjectionType ALL for secondary index
findings.description: The GSI1 global secondary index has projection_type = "ALL", which stores a copy of all attributes. If queries only need a subset, this wastes storage.
findings.fix_suggestion: Review GSI1 query patterns. Consider projection_type = "KEYS_ONLY" if only keys are needed.
findings.impact: Wastes storage costs (though minimal on PAY_PER_REQUEST).

findings.title: API Gateway stage throttling may be under-tuned
findings.description: The API Gateway $default stage has throttling configured (burst=200, rate=100 req/sec) but these settings are generic and not validated against actual application throughput.
findings.fix_suggestion: Monitor actual API Gateway request rate in CloudWatch and adjust throttle settings to match observed peak traffic + headroom.
findings.impact: If rate limits are set too low, legitimate traffic may be throttled; if too high, abuse isn't mitigated.

findings.title: S3 site bucket missing server-side encryption configuration
findings.description: The fuel-site S3 bucket does not have an explicit aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration block. While AWS S3 enables encryption by default since 2023, it's best practice to explicitly configure encryption to ensure it remains enabled and to document the security posture in code.
findings.impact: Objects in the bucket are encrypted by default, but the configuration is implicit rather than explicit, making it harder to audit and verify encryption compliance.

findings.title: API keys stored in Lambda environment variables instead of Secrets Manager
findings.description: The RESEND_API_KEY and USDA_API_KEY are passed to Lambda functions as environment variables. While the Anthropic API key is correctly stored in Secrets Manager, these two keys should also be managed through Secrets Manager to follow the principle of least privilege and prevent accidental exposure in logs or debugging output.
findings.impact: API keys are visible in Lambda environment variable listings, CloudWatch logs during debugging, and in the Terraform state file. This increases the attack surface for credential theft and violates security best practices for managing secrets.

findings.title: Lambda functions missing dead-letter queue (DLQ) configuration
findings.description: The API and AI Lambda functions are not configured with a dead-letter queue. Without a DLQ, failed Lambda invocations that exceed retry limits are silently dropped, making it impossible to investigate failures or implement retry logic.
findings.evidence:
resource "aws_lambda_function" "api" {
  function_name = "fuel-api"
  role          = aws_iam_role.lambda.arn
  handler       = "index.handler"
  runtime       = "nodejs20.x"
  architectures = ["arm64"]
  memory_size   = 256
  timeout       = 15
  # ... no dead_letter_config
}
findings.impact: Failed Lambda invocations cannot be recovered or debugged. Critical user requests may fail silently without alerting operations teams or providing a mechanism to retry.

findings.title: No CloudWatch alarms for Lambda error metrics
findings.description: Lambda functions lack CloudWatch alarms to alert on errors, throttles, and high duration. Without these alarms, operational issues in the API or AI functions may go unnoticed until users report problems.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Create CloudWatch alarms for Lambda metrics:

resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "api_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "fuel-api-errors"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = "1"
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  period              = "300"
  statistic           = "Sum"
  threshold           = "5"
  dimensions = {
    FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.api.function_name
  }
}

Add similar alarms for Throttles, Duration, and ConcurrentExecutions.
findings.impact: Operational blind spot: Lambda errors, throttles, and high execution times go unmonitored. On-call teams cannot be alerted to issues proactively.

findings.title: Missing CloudWatch alarms for API Gateway errors
findings.description: API Gateway stage does not have CloudWatch alarms configured to alert on 4xx and 5xx errors. This reduces visibility into API health and customer impact.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Create CloudWatch alarms for API Gateway:

resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "api_5xx_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "fuel-api-5xx-errors"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanOrEqualToThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = "1"
  metric_name         = "5XXError"
  namespace           = "AWS/ApiGateway"
  period              = "60"
  statistic           = "Sum"
  threshold           = "10"
  dimensions = {
    ApiName = aws_apigatewayv2_api.api.name
  }
}
findings.impact: API errors go undetected until customers report them. No alerting mechanism for high error rates.

findings.title: API behavior not compressing responses
findings.description: The /api/* cache behavior in CloudFront has compress = false. API responses (JSON) compress very well and should be gzip-compressed to reduce bandwidth and latency.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Change compress = false to compress = true in the /api/* cache behavior:

compress = true
findings.evidence:
ordered_cache_behavior {
  path_pattern           = "/api/*"
  allowed_methods        = ["DELETE", "GET", "HEAD", "OPTIONS", "PATCH", "POST", "PUT"]
  cached_methods         = ["GET", "HEAD"]
  target_origin_id       = "api-fuel"
  viewer_protocol_policy = "redirect-to-https"
  compress               = false  # ← Should be true
}
findings.impact: API responses are uncompressed, increasing bandwidth usage and latency for clients. For JSON APIs, compression typically reduces response size by 60-80%.

findings.title: Using deprecated forwarded_values instead of cache_policy_id
findings.description: The CloudFront default and ordered cache behaviors use the deprecated forwarded_values parameter instead of the newer cache_policy_id and origin_request_policy_id. While currently functional, AWS recommends migrating to the newer cache policies for better maintainability and future compatibility.
findings.impact: Technical debt: The current configuration uses a deprecated Terraform resource parameter that may eventually be removed. No immediate functional impact.

findings.title: Incomplete Terraform outputs for cross-stack reference
findings.description: The outputs.tf file exports only 3 values (CloudFront domain, API Gateway URL, S3 bucket name). For better operational awareness and to support cross-stack references, consider exporting additional values like Lambda ARNs, DynamoDB table ARN, and CloudWatch log group names.
findings.impact: Reduced operational visibility. Teams integrating with Fuel infrastructure must manually reference resource names rather than using Terraform outputs.

findings.title: Consider Lambda reserved concurrency for predictable workloads
findings.description: The API and AI Lambda functions do not have reserved concurrency configured. If fuel.codyjo.com experiences predictable traffic patterns, reserved concurrency could improve latency and reduce cost by guaranteeing capacity.
findings.impact: If traffic is predictable and peaks are regular, Lambda cold starts may impact latency. Reserved concurrency would ensure capacity and improve response times, but adds cost.

findings.title: RESEND_API_KEY exposed in plaintext Lambda environment variables
findings.description: The RESEND_API_KEY variable is passed directly to the Lambda function's environment variables in plaintext. Environment variables are visible in the AWS console, CloudWatch logs, X-Ray traces, and any error messages. Sensitive API keys should be stored in AWS Secrets Manager and retrieved at runtime, not stored in environment variables.
findings.impact: Resend API key is exposed in plaintext, accessible via AWS console, logs, traces, and error messages. This violates the principle of least privilege and increases risk of key compromise. The identical anthropic_api_key is correctly stored in Secrets Manager; RESEND_API_KEY should follow the same pattern.

findings.title: S3 site bucket missing explicit server-side encryption configuration
findings.description: The main site bucket (bible-app-site) does not have an explicit server-side encryption block. While AWS S3 encrypts all objects by default with SSE-S3, best practice for production assets is to explicitly configure encryption to ensure control and auditability. The access logs bucket also lacks explicit encryption.
findings.impact: Without explicit encryption configuration, there is no audit trail showing encryption is enforced. Compliance frameworks (SOC 2, ISO 27001) often require explicit encryption settings. Default encryption could theoretically be disabled at the AWS account level (though unlikely).

findings.title: No CloudWatch alarms configured for Lambda errors, API failures, or DynamoDB metrics
findings.description: The infrastructure lacks proactive monitoring alarms. There are no CloudWatch alarms for critical metrics like Lambda function errors, throttles, API Gateway 5xx errors, or DynamoDB consumed capacity. Without alarms, issues will only be discovered when users report them, delaying incident response.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Create CloudWatch alarms for:

1. Lambda errors:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "api_errors" {
  alarm_name          = "${var.project}-api-errors"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 1
  metric_name         = "Errors"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  period              = 60
  statistic           = "Sum"
  threshold           = 5
  dimensions = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.api.function_name }
  alarm_actions       = [aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn]
}

2. Lambda throttles:
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "api_throttles" {
  alarm_name          = "${var.project}-api-throttles"
  comparison_operator = "GreaterThanThreshold"
  evaluation_periods  = 1
  metric_name         = "Throttles"
  namespace           = "AWS/Lambda"
  period              = 60
  statistic           = "Sum"
  threshold           = 0
  dimensions = { FunctionName = aws_lambda_function.api.function_name }
}
findings.impact: Operational blind spot: errors won't trigger alerts, DynamoDB throttling won't be detected, API Gateway failures won't be visible. Mean time to detection (MTTD) increases from seconds to hours when issues occur. This is unacceptable for a production app used for daily Scripture reading.

findings.title: S3 site bucket has versioning enabled but no lifecycle policy for old versions
findings.description: The site bucket has S3 versioning enabled (line 13, s3.tf) to protect against accidental overwrites, but there is no lifecycle rule to expire or transition old versions. Over time, this will accumulate many old object versions, increasing storage costs without providing operational value.
findings.impact: Old versions of site assets (from CI/CD deploys) are retained indefinitely. If the site goes through multiple deployments per week, this could accumulate hundreds of versions over a year, adding to storage costs. Estimated cost: $0.023 per GB stored, so even 10 GB of old versions costs ~$2.30/month.

findings.title: CloudFront using deprecated forwarded_values instead of cache_policy_id
findings.description: The default and /api/* cache behaviors in CloudFront use the legacy forwarded_values block (cloudfront.tf lines 156-161, 177-182) instead of the modern cache_policy_id. AWS has deprecated forwarded_values in favor of explicit cache policies, which provide better control and clarity over caching logic.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Replace forwarded_values with cache_policy_id. Use AWS managed cache policies:

For S3 static content, use Managed-CachingOptimized:
default_cache_behavior {
  allowed_methods            = ["GET", "HEAD", "OPTIONS"]
  cached_methods             = ["GET", "HEAD"]
  target_origin_id           = "s3-${var.project}"
  viewer_protocol_policy     = "redirect-to-https"
  compress                   = true
  cache_policy_id            = "658327ea-f89d-4fab-a63d-7e88639e58f6"
  response_headers_policy_id = aws_cloudfront_response_headers_policy.security.id
}
findings.impact: Using deprecated forwarded_values may cause Terraform warnings and could be removed in future AWS provider versions. Modern cache policies provide clearer intent and are easier to audit. The current config works but is not future-proof.

findings.title: Terraform S3 backend missing DynamoDB state locking
findings.description: The Terraform backend configuration (main.tf lines 15-19) uses an S3 bucket for state storage but does not configure a DynamoDB table for state locking. Without locking, concurrent terraform operations could cause state corruption if multiple team members run terraform apply simultaneously.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Add DynamoDB state locking:

  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "photo-gallery-tfstate-229678440188"
    key            = "bible-app/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-west-2"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks"
    encrypt        = true
  }
findings.impact: If multiple developers or CI/CD pipelines run terraform concurrently, state corruption could occur, leading to infrastructure being deployed in an inconsistent state. For a team environment, this is a significant operational risk.

findings.title: Lambda functions missing explicit CloudWatch log group with retention policy
findings.description: The Lambda functions (api, interpret) rely on the implicit CloudWatch log group created by the AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole. There is no explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource with retention_in_days configured. Lambda logs will be retained indefinitely by default, increasing storage costs.
findings.impact: Lambda logs will be retained indefinitely, accumulating storage costs in CloudWatch Logs (~$0.50 per GB ingested). Over a year, this could add up to significant unnecessary costs.

findings.title: CloudFront CSP allows unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval, reducing XSS protection
findings.description: The Content Security Policy header (cloudfront.tf line 262) includes 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' in script-src. While this may be necessary for Next.js, it significantly weakens XSS (cross-site scripting) protection by allowing inline scripts, which are a common XSS vector.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Review whether 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' are truly necessary:

1. Check if Next.js build output can use style/script hashes instead of unsafe-inline.
2. If inline styles are unavoidable, use 'unsafe-inline' for style-src but keep script-src strict.
3. Remove 'unsafe-eval' unless explicitly required (check Next.js config).

Recommended CSP:
content_security_policy = "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' https://analytics.codyjo.com; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data: blob:; connect-src ${local.csp_connect_srcs}; font-src 'self'; frame-ancestors 'none'; base-uri 'self'; form-action 'self'"
findings.impact: The use of 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' allows any inline <script> or eval() call to execute, significantly reducing XSS protection. An attacker injecting a <script> tag or eval'd code could steal user data, modify the DOM, or execute malicious actions.

findings.title: Missing comprehensive cost allocation tags for multi-team billing
findings.description: The default_tags include Project, ManagedBy, and Environment, which is good. However, for detailed cost tracking in a multi-team organization, additional tags like CostCenter, Owner, or Application are best practice. This makes it easier to allocate infrastructure costs across teams.
findings.impact: Without fine-grained cost allocation tags, it's harder to track costs per team or cost center. This is primarily a billing/operations issue, not a functional one.

findings.title: CloudFront minimum TLS version could be updated to TLSv1.3
findings.description: The CloudFront distribution specifies minimum_protocol_version = "TLSv1.2_2021" (cloudfront.tf line 209). While TLSv1.2 is secure, TLSv1.3 is more modern and faster. AWS supports TLSv1.3 policy versions.
findings.impact: TLSv1.2 is secure and widely supported, so this is a low-priority improvement. TLSv1.3 offers better performance and stronger security.

findings.title: Lambda using inline IAM policies instead of managed policies
findings.description: The Lambda execution role uses inline policies. For better maintainability and reusability, permissions could be extracted into managed policies.
findings.fix_suggestion: This is a nice-to-have. If you want to refactor for clarity, extract into a managed policy that can be reused across multiple roles.
findings.impact: Inline policies are harder to reuse if another Lambda or role needs the same permissions. Not a critical issue for a small app, but limits flexibility.

findings.title: Resend API Key should be stored in Secrets Manager instead of environment variable
findings.description: Related to COPS-001, the Resend API key (for transactional email) is currently passed as an environment variable. Unlike ANTHROPIC_SECRET_ARN and JWT_SECRET_ARN which are correctly stored in Secrets Manager, RESEND_API_KEY should also be moved for consistency and security.
findings.fix_suggestion: See COPS-001 for the detailed fix. Store RESEND_API_KEY in Secrets Manager and pass the ARN as an environment variable, matching the pattern used for anthropic_api_key and jwt_secret.
findings.evidence: RESEND_API_KEY = var.resend_api_key  # Plaintext in environment
findings.impact: This is a duplicate/follow-up of COPS-001 (critical). The core issue is that RESEND_API_KEY is stored in plaintext, which is a security anti-pattern.

findings.title: API Lambda has overly broad DynamoDB permissions
findings.description: The API Lambda IAM role grants Scan and BatchWriteItem permissions on the entire DynamoDB table. While resources are correctly scoped, these permissions are broader than typical application needs. Most operations use GetItem, PutItem, Query, and DeleteItem. Scan is expensive and typically reserved for background operations. BatchWriteItem can be used for bulk mutations outside normal request handling.
findings.impact: If the Lambda is compromised, an attacker could perform full table scans (data exfiltration risk) or bulk mutations. Unnecessary permissions increase blast radius.

findings.title: Terraform state backend missing DynamoDB locking
findings.description: The S3 backend in main.tf does not specify a DynamoDB table for state locking. Without state locking, concurrent Terraform apply operations could cause state corruption or lost updates if multiple users/CI/CD pipelines run terraform apply simultaneously.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Create a DynamoDB table for Terraform state locking and add it to the backend configuration:

First, create the lock table (one-time setup):
aws dynamodb create-table \
  --table-name thenewbeautifulme-terraform-locks \
  --attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S \
  --key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH \
  --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST \
  --region us-west-2

Then update main.tf:
backend "s3" {
  bucket         = "photo-gallery-tfstate-229678440188"
  key            = "thenewbeautifulme/terraform.tfstate"
  region         = "us-west-2"
  dynamodb_table = "thenewbeautifulme-terraform-locks"
}
findings.impact: Race conditions during concurrent Terraform operations could lead to corrupted state, failed deployments, or inconsistent infrastructure. Critical for CI/CD with multiple pipelines.

findings.title: Analytics Lambda missing dead-letter queue (DLQ)
findings.description: The EventBridge scheduled Lambda invocation for analytics.tf does not have a dead-letter queue configured. If the Lambda fails, the event is dropped with no way to retry or investigate. For critical batch operations, a DLQ ensures observability and the ability to replay failed events.
findings.impact: If the analytics Lambda fails (e.g., due to a bug, timeout, or permission issue), the analytics aggregation is silently skipped. Over 6 hours, the dashboard will show stale data. Without a DLQ, the failure is unobservable.

findings.title: S3 uploads bucket missing server-side encryption configuration
findings.description: The uploads bucket in s3.tf does not explicitly enable server-side encryption with KMS. While AWS S3 defaults to SSE-S3, production buckets should explicitly enforce server-side encryption (ideally with customer-managed KMS keys) to ensure encryption keys are under your control and for compliance requirements.
findings.impact: User uploads (journal images) are encrypted at rest by default, but you have no control over encryption keys. For compliance (GDPR, HIPAA) and security audits, explicit encryption with customer-managed keys is preferred.

findings.title: API Lambda missing explicit CloudWatch log group resource
findings.description: The API Lambda function in lambda.tf does not have an explicit aws_cloudwatch_log_group resource defined. Lambda auto-creates log groups, but best practice is to explicitly manage them in Terraform for retention policies, encryption, and lifecycle control.
findings.evidence:
resource "aws_lambda_function" "api" {
  function_name                  = "${var.project}-api"
  role                           = aws_iam_role.lambda_api.arn
  handler                        = "index.handler"
  runtime                        = "nodejs20.x"
  architectures                  = ["arm64"]
  memory_size                    = 256
  timeout                        = 15
  reserved_concurrent_executions = 20
  filename                       = data.archive_file.api.output_path
  source_code_hash               = data.archive_file.api.output_base64sha256
  ...  # No explicit log group
}
findings.impact: Log retention is not explicitly managed; logs may accumulate indefinitely or be retained by AWS defaults. Without explicit Terraform management, it's harder to enforce retention policies across the organization.

findings.title: S3 uploads bucket CORS allows all headers
findings.description: The uploads bucket CORS configuration in s3.tf specifies allowed_headers = ["*"], which allows any HTTP header from the allowed origins. While the origins are correctly scoped to your domain, the wildcard headers are overly permissive.
findings.fix_suggestion:
Specify only the headers your application needs:

cors_rule {
  allowed_headers = ["Content-Type", "Authorization"]
  allowed_methods = ["PUT"]
  allowed_origins = ["https://${var.domain_name}", "https://www.${var.domain_name}"]
  max_age_seconds = 3600
}
findings.impact: Low risk. Browsers will still only send necessary headers, but this violates least-privilege principle. An attacker could probe for unexpected header handling.

findings.title: SNS topic subscription missing topic tag propagation
findings.description: The SNS topic in monitoring.tf has a Project tag, but the email subscription does not. For cost allocation and resource tracking, all resources should be consistently tagged.
findings.impact: Minor. SNS subscriptions don't incur separate costs, but for consistency and future cost allocation, tags should be applied.

findings.title: Hardcoded SNS email endpoint requires manual verification
findings.description: The SNS topic subscription in monitoring.tf has a hardcoded email endpoint 'alerts@thenewbeautifulme.com'. If this email changes or the recipient is wrong, alerts won't reach the ops team. SNS requires explicit email confirmation, which may have lapsed.
findings.impact: If the email address is incorrect or no longer monitored, critical alerts (Lambda errors, WAF blocks, throttles) won't notify the team.
